title: Wetting as an emergent property of water: reformulating Young equation on molecular grounds
abstract: Young equation provides a remarkably successful macroscopic description of wetting, yet its molecular origin (particularly for water) has remained elusive for over two centuries. Here we make the molecular basis of aqueous wetting explicit by reformulating it in terms of a molecular wetting coefficient, omega m, which quantifies how an interface compensates the intrinsic energetic cost of hydrogen-bond defects relative to bulk water. Across a broad and continuous spectrum of hydrophilicities, spanning chemically diverse experimental and model surfaces, macroscopic contact angles collapse onto a single universal master curve when expressed through omega m. This molecular reformulation closes Young and Young-Dupre relations on energetic grounds, establishing a unified and predictive physical link between wetting, adhesion, cavitation, and nanoconfined filling. By anchoring interfacial behavior to waters intrinsic hydrogen-bond energetic scales, our results reveal wetting as an emergent property of water itself, rather than a surface-specific attribute and provide a transferable molecular framework that recalibrates energetic intuition and guides the rational design of aqueous interfaces. (This document is the unedited Author version of a Submitted Manuscript subsequently accepted for publication in J. Am. Chem. Soc. For the published version, which includes a more complete molecular-thermodynamics grounding of the method see the published version)
summary: This work provides a molecular reformulation of wetting theory, moving beyond empirical macroscopic equations like Young's. By introducing a molecular wetting coefficient ($\omega_m$), the authors demonstrate that wetting is an emergent property dictated by water's internal hydrogen-bond energetics. This framework unifies wetting, adhesion, and cavitation into a single, predictive physical principle.
main_problem: The molecular origin of macroscopic wetting descriptions, such as Young's equation, particularly for water, has remained theoretically elusive.
main_result: Wetting is shown to be an emergent property of water's intrinsic hydrogen-bond energetic scales, unifying wetting, adhesion, and cavitation into a single molecular criterion.
method: The authors reformulate wetting using a molecular wetting coefficient ($\omega_m$) derived from microscopic interaction measures ($V_{4S}$) and test this framework using molecular dynamics simulations.
model_or_system: The system involves aqueous interfaces (solid-liquid-vapor, etc.) where the behavior is governed by the energetic cost associated with hydrogen-bond defects in the water network.
key_observables: Molecular wetting coefficient ($\omega_m$), contact angle ($\cos 	heta$), adhesion work ($W_{adh}$), and cavitation free energy ($\Delta G_{cav}$).
important_parameters: The molecular wetting coefficient ($\omega_m$), which quantifies the energetic compensation of HB defects relative to bulk water.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes that interfacial behavior can be mapped onto the intrinsic energetic scales of the water hydrogen-bond network, allowing for a universal description across different chemistries.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate that contact angles for diverse surfaces collapse onto a single master curve defined by $\cos 	heta = \omega_m$, and that thermodynamic quantities like cavitation energy show linear closure with $\omega_m$.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the macroscopic problem (Young's equation), introduces the molecular parameter ($\omega_m$), demonstrates its universality across various surfaces, and finally closes the thermodynamic loop by linking wetting, adhesion, and cavitation via $\omega_m$.
why_interesting: While focused on classical liquids, the rigorous thermodynamic closure and the development of a universal, microscopic parameter ($\omega_m$) to govern macroscopic phase behavior provides a powerful analogy for understanding emergent order in complex quantum many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Universal molecular description of aqueous wetting. Contact angles for chemically diverse systems plotted as a function of the molecular wetting coefficient ωm. Experimental data for self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) with different functionalization (from apolar to polar groups), together with results for dispersive graphene (with variable Lennard–Jones attractions) and polar silica (with varying polarity), collapse onto a single master curve defined by cos θ = ωm. Inset: Cavitation free energy for a small (methane-like sized) sphere tangent to the surface, −kBT ln P(N = 0), plotted against ωm for graphene and silica, demonstrating a thermodynamic closure linking wetting, adhesion,...

FIG. 2: Nanoconfinement, filling, and thermodynamic closure controlled by the molecular wetting coefficient. (a) Minimum stable plate separation Dmin for water confined between two parallel graphene sheets as a function of interaction strength ε. Water density profiles for 70%–9 ˚A and 80%–6.5 ˚A are sketched as insets. The green curve reports ωm for a single graphene surface; the vertical dashed green line (that extends down to the other two panels of the figure) marks the value of ε for which ωm = 0. The color of the symbols indicates the in-plane (2D) diffusion coefficient of confined water, from more mobile (red) to nearly arrested or frozen configurations (violet). (b) Molecular...
